examples.explanation: Start: s='aab', backtrack(0,0). Check substring s[0:1]='a': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(1,1). Check s[1:2]='a': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(2,2). Check s[2:3]='b': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(3,3). Base case: start=3, cuts=3, update min_cuts=2. Backtrack. Check s[1:3]='ab': not palindrome. Backtrack. Check s[0:2]='aa': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(2,1). Check s[2:3]='b': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(3,2). Base case: start=3, cuts=2, update min_cuts=1. Backtrack. Check s[0:3]='aab': not palindrome. End. Return min_cuts=1.

Start: s='abc', backtrack(0,0). Check s[0:1]='a': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(1,1). Check s[1:2]='b': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(2,2). Check s[2:3]='c': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(3,3). Base case: cuts=3, update min_cuts=2. Backtrack. Check s[1:3]='bc': not palindrome. Backtrack. Check s[0:2]='ab': not palindrome. Check s[0:3]='abc': not palindrome. End. Return min_cuts=2.

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible partitions of the string using recursion, check each substring for palindrome, and track the minimum cuts. This approach explores every combination but is inefficient for long strings.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Palindrome Partition Cuts via Brute Force Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion to explore all partitions and find minimum cuts.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the palindrome partitioning II problem. The intuition is that we need to partition the string into palindromic substrings with the fewest cuts. Since we don't know where the optimal cuts are, we must explore all possible partition points. We use recursion to systematically try every possible cut position. At each recursive step, we consider cutting after the current position if the substring from the last cut to current position is a palindrome. If it is, we make a cut and recurse. If not, we extend the substring. We track the minimum cuts found across all recursive paths. The algorithm starts with index 0 and no cuts, recursively explores all valid partitions, and returns the minimum cuts needed. Time complexity is O(n * 2^n) because there are 2^(n-1) possible partition points and checking each substring palindrome takes O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L1 by using the palindrome checking function. Common mistakes include not properly tracking the current partition count, forgetting to add 1 when making a cut, or not handling the base case correctly. Real code pattern: def min_cut_brute(s): min_cuts = [float('inf')]; def backtrack(start, cuts): if start == len(s): min_cuts[0] = min(min_cuts[0], cuts-1); return; for end in range(start+1, len(s)+1): if is_palindrome(s[start:end]): backtrack(end, cuts+1); backtrack(0, 0); return min_cuts[0].

examples.trace: Start: s='aab', backtrack(0,0). Check substring s[0:1]='a': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(1,1). Check s[1:2]='a': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(2,2). Check s[2:3]='b': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(3,3). Base case: start=3, cuts=3, update min_cuts=2. Backtrack. Check s[1:3]='ab': not palindrome. Backtrack. Check s[0:2]='aa': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(2,1). Check s[2:3]='b': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(3,2). Base case: start=3, cuts=2, update min_cuts=1. Backtrack. Check s[0:3]='aab': not palindrome. End. Return min_cuts=1.

Start: s='abc', backtrack(0,0). Check s[0:1]='a': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(1,1). Check s[1:2]='b': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(2,2). Check s[2:3]='c': palindrome. Recurse backtrack(3,3). Base case: cuts=3, update min_cuts=2. Backtrack. Check s[1:3]='bc': not palindrome. Backtrack. Check s[0:2]='ab': not palindrome. Check s[0:3]='abc': not palindrome. End. Return min_cuts=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Palindrome Checking Function
ladders.desc: Function to determine if a substring is a palindrome.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function that checks whether a given string is a palindrome. The intuition is that a palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards, so we can verify this by comparing characters from both ends moving toward the center. The algorithm takes a string as input and uses two pointers: one starting at the beginning (left) and one at the end (right). While left < right, compare characters at these positions. If any pair doesn't match, return False. If all pairs match, return True. Time complexity is O(n) where n is string length, as we examine each character once. Space complexity is O(1) as we use only pointers. This connects to L0 by being called repeatedly in the backtracking to validate partitions. Common mistakes include incorrect pointer initialization, off-by-one errors in loop conditions, or not handling empty strings properly. Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s): left, right = 0, len(s)-1; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: return False; left += 1; right -= 1; return True.

examples.trace: Input: 'racecar'. left=0 ('r'), right=6 ('r'): match. left=1 ('a'), right=5 ('a'): match. left=2 ('c'), right=4 ('c'): match. left=3 ('e'), right=3: loop ends. Return True.
Input: 'hello'. left=0 ('h'), right=4 ('o'): mismatch. Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Backtracking for Partition Exploration
ladders.desc: Recursive function to generate all possible partitions of a string.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique of generating all possible ways to cut a string into substrings. The intuition is that partitioning a string is like deciding at each position whether to cut or not, leading to an exponential number of combinations. We use recursion to explore these combinations systematically. The algorithm takes a string s, a start index, and a current cuts count. Base case: if start equals string length, we've reached the end of a partition path. Otherwise, for each possible end index from start+1 to len(s), we consider the substring s[start:end]. We then recurse to explore further partitions from the end index. This generates all partitions by trying every possible cut point at each step. Time complexity is O(2^n) as there are 2^(n-1) possible partitions. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L0 by forming the main recursive structure that integrates palindrome checking. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling, not updating indices properly, or infinite recursion. Real code pattern: def backtrack(start, cuts): if start == len(s): process partition; return; for end in range(start+1, len(s)+1): backtrack(end, cuts+1).

examples.trace: Start: backtrack(0,0). end=1: substring='a', recurse backtrack(1,1). Base case: partition=['a','b']. Backtrack. end=2: substring='ab', recurse backtrack(2,1). Base case: partition=['ab']. End.

Start: backtrack(0,0). end=1: recurse backtrack(1,1). From backtrack(1,1): end=2: recurse backtrack(2,2). Base: ['a','b','c']. Backtrack: end=3: recurse backtrack(3,2). Base: ['a','bc']. Backtrack. end=2: recurse backtrack(2,1). From backtrack(2,1): end=3: recurse backtrack(3,2). Base: ['ab','c']. Backtrack. end=3: recurse backtrack(3,1). Base: ['abc']. End.

ladders.title: Building Block — String Slicing and Substring Extraction
ladders.desc: Extracting substrings from a string using indices.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves accessing portions of a string by specifying start and end indices. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can isolate contiguous segments by defining a range. The algorithm uses slicing notation s[start:end] to get characters from index start (inclusive) to end (exclusive). For example, in Python, s[0:3] gets the first three characters. We need this to check potential palindrome substrings during partitioning. Time complexity is O(k) where k is substring length, as it creates a new string. Space complexity is O(k) for the new substring. This connects to L2 by providing the substrings that are checked in backtracking. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using end index inclusive instead of exclusive), negative indices, or slicing beyond string bounds. Real code pattern: substring = s[start:end].

examples.trace: String: 'hello' indices: 0='h',1='e',2='l',3='l',4='o'. Slice s[1:4] takes characters at indices 1,2,3: 'e','l','l' → 'ell'.
String: 'algorithm' indices: 0='a',1='l',2='g',3='o',4='r',5='i',6='t',7='h',8='m'. Slice s[0:5] takes indices 0-4: 'a','l','g','o','r' → 'algor'.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison in a String
ladders.desc: Comparing individual characters at specific positions in a string.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing and comparing characters in a string using their indices. The intuition is that strings are indexed starting from 0, and we can retrieve any character by its position. The algorithm uses bracket notation s[i] to get the character at index i. We compare characters using equality operators (== or !=). This is essential for palindrome checking where we compare characters from opposite ends. Time complexity is O(1) for accessing and comparing. Space complexity is O(1) as no extra storage is needed. This connects to L3 by being the core operation in substring palindrome validation. Common mistakes include index out of bounds errors, confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based, or comparing characters incorrectly (e.g., case sensitivity). Real code pattern: if s[i] == s[j]: ... else: ... .

examples.trace: String: 'hello'. s[1] = 'e', s[3] = 'l'. Compare 'e' == 'l'? False.
String: 'racecar'. s[0] = 'r', s[6] = 'r'. Compare 'r' == 'r'? True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understanding the definition and examples of palindromes.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a palindrome is a sequence that reads the same forwards and backwards. The intuition is symmetry in characters. For strings, it means the first character equals the last, second equals second-last, and so on. Examples include single characters (always palindromes), 'racecar', 'madam', or 'abba'. Non-examples include 'hello', 'world'. This foundational idea is crucial because the problem requires partitioning into palindromic substrings. We'll use this to validate partitions. Time and space analysis aren't applicable here as it's a definition. This connects to L4 by providing the reasoning for character comparisons. Common mistakes include thinking palindromes must be words (they can be any sequence), or confusing with other patterns like anagrams. Real code pattern: Concept only, no code.

examples.trace: Read forwards: r-a-c-e-c-a-r. Read backwards: r-a-c-e-c-a-r. They are identical.
Read forwards: h-e-l-l-o. Read backwards: o-l-l-e-h. They are different.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Palindrome Check O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use DP to store minimum cuts for prefixes and precompute palindrome status for all substrings. This reduces redundant checks by building solutions incrementally from smaller subproblems.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Cuts for Palindrome Partitioning
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP with precomputed palindrome table to find minimum cuts needed to partition a string into palindromic substrings.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires partitioning a string such that every substring is a palindrome, with the goal of minimizing the number of cuts. The brute-force approach would involve checking all possible partitions, which is exponential. The optimized solution uses dynamic programming with two tables: one for palindrome checks and one for minimum cuts. The intuition is to build solutions incrementally: for each position i in the string, we compute the minimum cuts needed for the prefix ending at i by considering all possible palindrome substrings ending at i. We precompute a 2D boolean table 'pal' where pal[i][j] is true if substring s[i:j+1] is a palindrome. This is done using DP with expansion from centers or bottom-up filling. Then, we compute cuts[i] = minimum cuts for s[0:i+1]. Initialize cuts[i] = i (worst case: cut between every character). For each i, if pal[0][i] is true, cuts[i] = 0 (entire prefix is palindrome). Otherwise, for j from 0 to i-1, if pal[j+1][i] is true, then cuts[i] = min(cuts[i], cuts[j] + 1). This works because if substring s[j+1:i+1] is palindrome, we can cut after j, adding 1 cut to cuts[j]. Time complexity is O(n²) for building both tables, space is O(n²) for the palindrome table and O(n) for cuts array. Connection to L1: This level integrates the palindrome checking subroutine from L1 into the full DP solution. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, not initializing cuts properly, or inefficient palindrome checks. Real code pattern: def minCut(s): n = len(s); pal = [[False]*n for _ in range(n)]; for i in range(n): pal[i][i] = True; for length from 2 to n: for i from 0 to n-length: j = i+length-1; if s[i]==s[j] and (length==2 or pal[i+1][j-1]): pal[i][j]=True; cuts = [0]*n; for i in range(n): minCuts = i; for j in range(i): if pal[j+1][i]: minCuts = min(minCuts, cuts[j]+1); cuts[i] = minCuts; return cuts[n-1].

examples.trace: s = 'aab', n=3. Build pal table: pal[0][0]=true, pal[1][1]=true, pal[2][2]=true, pal[0][1]=true ('aa'), pal[1][2]=false, pal[0][2]=false. cuts[0]=0. For i=1: pal[0][1]=true → cuts[1]=0. For i=2: pal[0][2]=false; check j=0 (pal[1][2]=false), j=1 (pal[2][2]=true) → cuts[2] = min(2, cuts[1]+1) = min(2, 0+1) = 1. Final answer: 1.
s = 'abcba'. Entire string is a palindrome (pal[0][4]=true) → cuts[4]=0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Precomputing Palindrome Table
ladders.desc: Build a 2D DP table to store whether all substrings are palindromes in O(n²) time.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine precomputes a boolean table 'pal' where pal[i][j] indicates if substring s[i:j+1] is a palindrome. The intuition is to avoid redundant checks by using dynamic programming: a substring is palindrome if its first and last characters match and the inner substring is also palindrome. We build this table bottom-up by length. Start with base cases: single characters are palindromes (pal[i][i] = true). For length 2, check if s[i] == s[i+1]. For longer lengths, pal[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) && pal[i+1][j-1]. This requires filling in increasing order of length to ensure pal[i+1][j-1] is already computed. Alternatively, we can expand from centers, but DP is straightforward. Time complexity is O(n²) as we iterate over all O(n²) substrings. Space complexity is O(n²) for the table. Connection to L0: This table is used in the main DP to quickly check if a substring is palindrome without recomputing. Common mistakes include incorrect indices leading to out-of-bounds access, not handling length 2 separately, or filling in wrong order. Real code pattern: n = len(s); pal = [[False]*n for _ in range(n)]; for i in range(n): pal[i][i] = True; for length in range(2, n+1): for i in range(n-length+1): j = i+length-1; if length == 2: pal[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]); else: pal[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j] and pal[i+1][j-1]).

examples.trace: String: abba, n=4. Initialize pal all False. Set diagonals: pal[0][0]=T, pal[1][1]=T, pal[2][2]=T, pal[3][3]=T. Length 2: i=0,j=1: s[0]=a,s[1]=b -> F; i=1,j=2: s[1]=b,s[2]=b -> T; i=2,j=3: s[2]=b,s[3]=a -> F. Length 3: i=0,j=2: s[0]=a,s[2]=b -> F; i=1,j=3: s[1]=b,s[3]=a -> F. Length 4: i=0,j=3: s[0]=a,s[3]=a and pal[1][2]=T -> T. Final table: pal[0][0]=T, pal[0][1]=F, pal[0][2]=F, pal[0][3]=T; pal[1][1]=T, pal[1][2]=T, pal[1][3]=F; pal[2][2]=T, pal[2][3]=F; pal[3][3]=T.

String: abc, n=3. Initialize: pal[0][0]=T, pal[1][1]=T, pal[2][2]=T. Length 2: i=0,j=1: s[0]=a,s[1]=b -> F; i=1,j=2: s[1]=b,s[2]=c -> F. Length 3: i=0,j=2: s[0]=a,s[2]=c -> F. All other entries False.

testCases.expected: [[True, False, True], [False, True, False], [False, False, True]]
[[True, False, False, True], [False, True, True, False], [False, False, True, False], [False, False, False, True]]
[[True, False, False, False, True], [False, True, False, True, False], [False, False, True, False, False], [False, False, False, True, False], [False, False, False, False, True]]
[[True, False, False, True], [False, True, True, False], [False, False, True, False], [False, False, False, True]]
[[True, False, False, False, True], [False, True, False, True, False], [False, False, True, False, False], [False, False, False, True, False], [False, False, False, False, True]]

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP for Minimum Cuts Using Precomputed Table
ladders.desc: Compute minimum cuts for each prefix using the palindrome table to find optimal partitions.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the dynamic programming logic to compute cuts[i], the minimum cuts needed for substring s[0:i+1]. The intuition is to build the solution incrementally: for each position i, we consider all possible last palindrome substrings ending at i. If the entire prefix s[0:i+1] is palindrome, cuts[i] = 0. Otherwise, we try all possible cut points j before i: if substring s[j+1:i+1] is palindrome, then we can cut after j, making total cuts = cuts[j] + 1. We take the minimum over all such j. This works because cuts[j] gives optimal cuts for prefix ending at j, and adding a palindrome after it requires one additional cut. Initialize cuts[i] = i as worst case (cut between every character). The DP transition is: cuts[i] = min(cuts[i], cuts[j] + 1) for all j where pal[j+1][i] is true. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops over i and j. Space is O(n) for cuts array. Connection to L1: Uses the precomputed palindrome table from L1 for O(1) checks. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization, off-by-one in indices (e.g., using pal[j][i] instead of pal[j+1][i]), or not checking the entire prefix first. Real code pattern: cuts = [0]*n; for i in range(n): if pal[0][i]: cuts[i] = 0; else: minCuts = i; for j in range(i): if pal[j+1][i]: minCuts = min(minCuts, cuts[j] + 1); cuts[i] = minCuts; return cuts[n-1].

examples.input: cuts array build for "aab" with pal table
examples.trace: String: aab, n=3. Assume pal table from L1: pal[0][0]=T, pal[0][1]=T, pal[0][2]=F, pal[1][1]=T, pal[1][2]=F, pal[2][2]=T. Initialize cuts size 3. i=0: pal[0][0]=T -> cuts[0]=0. i=1: pal[0][1]=T -> cuts[1]=0. i=2: pal[0][2]=F, so minCuts=2; j=0: pal[1][2]=F; j=1: pal[2][2]=T -> minCuts=min(2, cuts[1]+1=0+1=1)=1; cuts[2]=1. So cuts = [0,0,1].

examples.input: cuts array build for "abcd" with pal table
examples.trace: String: abcd, n=4. pal table: only diagonals true. cuts[0]=0. i=1: pal[0][1]=F, minCuts=1; j=0: pal[1][1]=T -> minCuts=min(1, cuts[0]+1=1)=1; cuts[1]=1. i=2: pal[0][2]=F, minCuts=2; j=0: pal[1][2]=F; j=1: pal[2][2]=T -> minCuts=min(2, cuts[1]+1=2)=2; cuts[2]=2. i=3: pal[0][3]=F, minCuts=3; j=0: pal[1][3]=F; j=1: pal[2][3]=F; j=2: pal[3][3]=T -> minCuts=min(3, cuts[2]+1=3)=3; cuts[3]=3.

testCases.input: pal: [[True, False, True], [False, True, False], [False, False, True]], cuts for "aba"
pal: [[True, False, False, True], [False, True, True, False], [False, False, True, False], [False, False, False, True]], cuts for "abba"

pal: [[True, False, False, False, True], [False, True, False, True, False], [False, False, True, False, False], [False, False, False, True, False], [False, False, False, False, True]], cuts for "abcba"
pal: all diagonals true only, cuts for "abcde"
pal: [[True, False, False, False, False, True], ...], cuts for "racecar"
pal: [[True, False, False, True], [False, True, True, False], [False, False, True, False], [False, False, False, True]], cuts for "noon"

pal: [[True, False, False, False, True], [False, True, False, True, False], [False, False, True, False, False], [False, False, False, True, False], [False, False, False, False, True]], cuts for "level"

ladders.title: Building Block — Palindrome Check for a Substring
ladders.desc: Determine if a given substring is a palindrome using two-pointer expansion.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves checking whether a substring s[left:right+1] is a palindrome. The intuition is that a palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards, so we can compare characters from both ends moving inward. A simple approach uses two pointers: start at left and right, while left < right, if s[left] != s[right], return false; otherwise, increment left and decrement right. If all comparisons match, return true. This runs in O(k) time where k is substring length, and O(1) space. This is a fundamental operation used in building the palindrome table, though in the full solution we optimize with DP. Connection to L2: This check is the basis for filling the palindrome table; in DP, we use it to set base cases and derive longer palindromes. Common mistakes include incorrect bounds, not handling empty or single-character substrings, or inefficiency when called repeatedly. Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s, left, right): while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: return False; left += 1; right -= 1; return True.

examples.trace: Check substring 'racecar': left=0, right=6 -> s[0]=r, s[6]=r, match. left=1, right=5 -> s[1]=a, s[5]=a, match. left=2, right=4 -> s[2]=c, s[4]=c, match. left=3, right=3 -> loop ends, return true.
Check substring 'ell': left=1, right=3 -> s[1]=e, s[3]=l, mismatch, return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Indexing and Slicing
ladders.desc: Access characters and substrings in a string using indices and slices.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves working with string indices to retrieve characters or substrings. In most programming languages, strings are indexed starting from 0. For a string s of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. Accessing s[i] gives the character at position i. Slicing s[i:j] gives substring from index i inclusive to j exclusive. If j is omitted, it goes to end; if i is omitted, it starts from beginning. Negative indices count from the end. Understanding this is crucial for palindrome checks and DP indices. Time complexity for indexing is O(1), for slicing O(k) where k is slice length. Space for slice is O(k). Connection to L3: This operation is used in palindrome checks to compare characters or extract substrings. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors, accessing out-of-bounds indices, or misunderstanding slice boundaries. Real code pattern: s = "hello"; char = s[0] # 'h'; substring = s[1:4] # 'ell'; full = s[:] # 'hello'.

examples.trace: String 'algorithm' indices: 0:a,1:l,2:g,3:o,4:r,5:i,6:t,7:h,8:m. s[2] is character at index 2: 'g'. s[2:5] is substring from index 2 inclusive to 5 exclusive: indices 2,3,4 -> 'gor'. s[-1] is last character: index 8 -> 'm'.

String 'dynamic': indices 0:d,1:y,2:n,3:a,4:m,5:i,6:c. s[1:6:2] means start at 1, end before 6, step 2: indices 1,3,5 -> characters y, a, i? Wait, index 5 is 'i', but output says 'yac'. Let's correct: s[1:6] gives 'ynami', step 2 takes indices 1,3,5 -> y, a, i -> 'yai'. But expected 'yac' suggests maybe string is 'dynamic'? Actually, 'dynamic' has indices: 0:d,1:y,2:n,3:a,4:m,5:i,6:c? No, 'dynamic' is 7 letters: d-y-n-a-m-i-c. So indices 0:d,1:y,2:n,3:a,4:m,5:i,6:c. s[1:6] is indices 1 to 5: y,n,a,m,i -> 'ynami'. Step 2: indices 1,3,5 -> y,a,i -> 'yai'. But expected 'yac' might be a different example. Let's use a clear example: s="python", s[1:5:2] -> indices 1,3 -> y,h -> 'yh'. So for this input, I'll provide a generic trace: For s="example", s[1:6:2] -> indices 1,3,5 -> x,m,l -> 'xml'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition and examples of palindromes in strings.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a sequence that reads the same forwards and backwards. For strings, this means the string is identical when reversed. Examples include single characters (e.g., 'a'), words like 'racecar' or 'level', and phrases ignoring spaces/punctuation like 'A man a plan a canal Panama'. The key property is symmetry: the first character equals the last, second equals second-last, etc. This concept is foundational for the problem because we need to partition the string into palindromic substrings. Understanding palindromes helps in designing checks and algorithms. Connection to L4: This concept uses string operations to compare characters. Common mistakes include case sensitivity (palindromes are usually case-sensitive in this problem), ignoring non-alphanumeric characters (not relevant here), or misunderstanding empty strings (considered palindrome by definition). Real code pattern: A string s is palindrome if s == s[::-1] (reversed string).

examples.trace: String 'madam': forward: m-a-d-a-m, backward: m-a-d-a-m. They are identical, so true.
String 'hello': forward: h-e-l-l-o, backward: o-l-l-e-h. They differ, so false.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Center Expansion O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Compute palindrome substrings using center expansion to fill a DP array for cuts. This optimizes space by avoiding a full 2D table and focuses on expanding from centers to find palindromes efficiently.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Palindrome Partition Cuts with Center Expansion
ladders.desc: Complete solution using dynamic programming with center expansion to find minimum cuts needed to partition a string into palindromic substrings.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires partitioning a string into palindromic substrings with the minimum number of cuts. The optimal approach combines center expansion for palindrome detection with dynamic programming for cut optimization. The intuition is that we can efficiently find all palindromic substrings by expanding from centers (both odd and even length), then use a DP array to track minimum cuts up to each position. For each position i, we initialize cuts[i] = i (worst case: cut between every character). Then for each palindrome found via center expansion (from l to r), we update cuts[r] = min(cuts[r], cuts[l-1] + 1) if l > 0, else cuts[r] = 0 (no cut needed for palindrome starting at beginning). The algorithm: 1. Initialize cuts array with worst-case values. 2. For each center (2n-1 possibilities), expand outward while characters match. 3. For each valid palindrome [l, r], update cuts[r] using the DP relation. 4. Return cuts[n-1]. Time: O(n²) from center expansion (each expansion can go up to n/2). Space: O(n) for cuts array. Connection to L1: This integrates the palindrome detection subroutine with DP optimization. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle even-length centers properly, not updating cuts correctly when l=0, or using inefficient palindrome checking. Real code pattern: Initialize cuts = [i for i in range(n)]. For center in range(2*n-1): l = center//2; r = l + center%2; while l>=0 and r<n and s[l]==s[r]: if l==0: cuts[r]=0; else: cuts[r]=min(cuts[r], cuts[l-1]+1); l-=1; r+=1. Return cuts[-1].

examples.trace: s = 'aab', n=3, cuts = [0,1,2]. Center expansion finds: (0,0) 'a' → cuts[0]=0; (0,1) 'aa' → cuts[1]=0; (2,2) 'b' → cuts[2]=min(2, cuts[1]+1)=1. Final cuts=[0,0,1] → return 1.
s = 'abba'. Center expansion finds full string palindrome (0,3) → cuts[3]=0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Center Expansion for Palindrome Detection
ladders.desc: Find all palindromic substrings in a string by expanding from centers.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine detects all palindromic substrings efficiently by treating each position as a center and expanding outward while characters match. The intuition is that any palindrome has a center (for odd length) or between two characters (for even length). By iterating through 2n-1 possible centers (n for odd, n-1 for even), we can find all palindromes in O(n²) time worst-case but often faster. For each center, set left l = center//2 and right r = l + center%2. While l >= 0 and r < n and s[l] == s[r], we have a palindrome from l to r. Decrement l and increment r to expand. This avoids precomputing a 2D table, saving space. Time: O(n²) in worst case (e.g., all same character), but average may be better. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This provides the palindrome information needed to update the DP cuts array. Common mistakes: Incorrect center indexing for even lengths, not handling empty string, or expanding beyond bounds. Real code pattern: For center in range(2*n-1): l = center//2; r = l + center%2; while l>=0 and r<n and s[l]==s[r]: # palindrome found, process it; l-=1; r+=1.

examples.trace: n=3. Centers 0-5. Center 0 (l=0,r=0): s[0]=a, palindrome 'a', expand l=-1 stop. Center 1 (l=0,r=1): s[0]=a, s[1]=b, no match. Center 2 (l=1,r=1): 'b', expand l=0,r=2: s[0]=a, s[2]=c, no. Center 3 (l=1,r=2): s[1]=b, s[2]=c, no. Center 4 (l=2,r=2): 'c', expand l=1,r=3 stop. Center 5 (l=2,r=3 invalid). Total: 3 palindromes.

n=3. Center 0: l=0,r=0, 'a', expand l=-1. Center 1: l=0,r=1, s[0]=a, s[1]=b, no. Center 2: l=1,r=1, 'b', expand l=0,r=2: s[0]=a, s[2]=a, match, palindrome 'aba', expand l=-1,r=3 stop. Center 3: l=1,r=2, s[1]=b, s[2]=a, no. Center 4: l=2,r=2, 'a', expand l=1,r=3 stop. Center 5 invalid. Palindromes: a, b, a, aba.

testCases.expected: Palindromes: n, o, o, n, noo? Actually: Center 0: n; Center 1: l=0,r=1 n!=o; Center 2: o; Center 3: l=1,r=2 o==o, palindrome 'oo', expand l=0,r=3 n==n, palindrome 'noon'; Center 4: o; Center 5: l=2,r=3 o!=n; Center 6: n. So: n, o, o, n, oo, noon.
Palindromes: a, b, b, a, bb, abba
Palindromes: a, b, c, b, a, bcb, abcba
Palindromes: a, a, a, aa, aa, aaa
Palindromes: a, b, c, d, c, b, a, cdc, bcdcb, abcdcba

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dynamic Programming Relation for Minimum Cuts
ladders.desc: Define and apply the DP recurrence to compute minimum cuts using palindrome information.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that if we know all palindromic substrings, we can use dynamic programming to find the minimum cuts. Let cuts[i] be the minimum cuts needed for substring s[0..i]. Base case: cuts[0] = 0 (single character is palindrome). For i from 1 to n-1, initialize cuts[i] = i (worst case: cut between every character). Then, for each palindrome from l to r (where l <= r), we update cuts[r] = min(cuts[r], cuts[l-1] + 1) if l > 0, meaning we cut before this palindrome and add one cut. If l == 0, then cuts[r] = 0 because the entire prefix is a palindrome. The intuition is that we build solutions incrementally: to reach position r, we consider ending with a palindrome starting at l, and add a cut before it (except when it starts at the beginning). This ensures optimal substructure. Time: O(n²) if we process all O(n²) palindromes. Space: O(n) for cuts array. Connection to L1: This uses the palindrome list from center expansion. Common mistakes: Not initializing cuts properly, forgetting the l=0 case, or updating cuts in wrong order. Real code pattern: cuts = [i for i in range(n)]; for each palindrome (l,r): if l==0: cuts[r]=0; else: cuts[r]=min(cuts[r], cuts[l-1]+1).

examples.trace: n=3, cuts init [0,1,2]. Palindrome (0,0): l=0, cuts[0]=0. (1,1): l=1, cuts[1]=min(1, cuts[0]+1=1)=1. (2,2): l=2, cuts[2]=min(2, cuts[1]+1=2)=2. (0,1): l=0, cuts[1]=0. Re-check (1,1) with updated cuts[0]=0: cuts[1]=min(0, cuts[0]+1=1)=0. Then (2,2): cuts[2]=min(2, cuts[1]+1=1)=1. Final cuts=[0,0,1].

ladders.title: Building Block — Palindrome Checking with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Determine if a substring is a palindrome by comparing characters from ends inward.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept checks whether a given substring s[l..r] is a palindrome using two pointers. The intuition is that a palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards, so we can compare characters from the ends moving inward until they meet. Start with left = l and right = r. While left < right, if s[left] != s[right], return false; otherwise, increment left and decrement right. If loop completes, return true. This runs in O(k) time where k = r-l+1 is substring length, and uses O(1) space. Connection to L2: This is a simpler version of center expansion; center expansion optimizes by finding all palindromes at once rather than checking each substring individually. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices, not handling empty or single-character substrings correctly (they are palindromes). Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s, l, r): while l < r: if s[l] != s[r]: return False; l+=1; r-=1; return True.

examples.trace: l=0, r=6, s[0]=r, s[6]=r, match, l=1,r=5: s[1]=a, s[5]=a, match, l=2,r=4: s[2]=c, s[4]=c, match, l=3,r=3: stop (l<r false), return true.
l=1, r=3, s[1]=e, s[3]=l, no match, return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Character Access and Comparison
ladders.desc: Access and compare individual characters in a string using indices.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves retrieving characters from a string by their index and comparing them for equality. In most programming languages, strings are indexed starting from 0. For a string s of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. Accessing s[i] returns the character at position i. Comparison uses equality operators (e.g., s[i] == s[j]). The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can examine them piece by piece to detect patterns like palindromes. Time: O(1) per access/comparison. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This is used in the two-pointer palindrome check to compare characters. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds (accessing beyond n-1), confusing 0-based indexing, or not handling empty strings. Real code pattern: if i < len(s) and j < len(s): if s[i] == s[j]: # do something.

examples.trace: String "hello" has indices: 0:h, 1:e, 2:l, 3:l, 4:o. Access s[1] gives 'e', s[4] gives 'o'. Compare 'e' == 'o' returns false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition and examples of palindromes.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a sequence that reads the same forwards and backwards. In the context of strings, it means the string is identical when reversed. Examples: "racecar", "madam", "noon", "a", "aa". Non-examples: "hello", "abc". The intuition is symmetry: the first character equals the last, second equals second-last, etc. This property is key to the partitioning problem because we want to cut the string into pieces that are all palindromes. For beginners, think of words like "radar" or numbers like "12321". Empty string is typically considered a palindrome (reads same forward/backward). Single characters are always palindromes. Connection to L4: This concept motivates why we compare characters from ends inward. Common mistakes: Confusing palindromes with other patterns, or not considering even-length palindromes like "abba". Real code pattern: A string s is palindrome if s == s[::-1] in Python.

examples.trace: Reverse "racecar" is "racecar", same as original. Check characters: r=r, a=a, c=c, e=e, c=c, a=a, r=r.
Reverse "hello" is "olleh", different from "hello". Check: h vs o, e vs l, etc.
